name: Aaru
description: Aaru, also known as the Field of Reeds or the Field of Offerings, is a paradisiacal realm in ancient Egyptian mythology. It is depicted as a lush and fertile land, a mirror image of the mortal world but infinitely more bountiful and pleasant. Aaru is the final destination for worthy souls who successfully navigate the perils of the Duat, the Egyptian underworld, and pass the weighing of the heart ceremony conducted by Anubis and Osiris. Only those whose hearts are lighter than the feather of Ma'at, signifying a life lived in truth and justice, are granted access to this idyllic afterlife. In Aaru, the deceased continue their existence, enjoying the fruits of their labor and living in harmony with nature and the gods. They cultivate the land, engage in leisurely activities, and are reunited with loved ones. The specifics of Aaru varied in different periods of Egyptian history and across different regions, but the central theme remained consistent: a perfect reflection of life, free from suffering and filled with abundance. It represented the ultimate reward for a virtuous life and the promise of eternal happiness. Some depictions show the deceased performing agricultural tasks, which were considered essential to maintaining the balance of the cosmos, highlighting the importance of contributing to the world even in the afterlife. Aaru was not simply a passive state of bliss but an active continuation of life, albeit in a perfected form, ensuring sustenance and joy for eternity. This ideal afterlife motivated individuals to live morally upright lives, striving to achieve Ma'at and earn their place in the blessed fields of Aaru.
subtitle: Egyptian Paradise, Field of Reeds

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: The deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at. If the heart is lighter, the soul is deemed worthy and allowed to enter Aaru.

keyMyths.title: Life in Aaru
keyMyths.description: Souls in Aaru cultivate the land and live in harmony. They enjoy eternal sustenance and reunite with loved ones.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.work: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.description: A comprehensive translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.
sources.text: A comprehensive translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.
